title: Inside-Cabinet Cutting Board Storage Rack
summary: A slim rack that mounts inside a cabinet door and holds cutting boards upright instead of leaning loose against pots and pans.
description:
Cutting boards stored flat get buried under pots and pans, and stored loose on their edge they slide and scratch each other. This rack solves both problems by giving each board its own upright slot, mounted on the inside of a cabinet door where it uses space that's otherwise empty.

At roughly 14-1/2" wide and 8" tall, it's sized to fit **inside a standard cabinet door** without interfering with the door's swing or the shelves behind it — measure your own door before committing to these dimensions if your cabinets run smaller.

Every piece is cut from a single 1x4 board (a true 1/2" thick, not full 3/4" stock), which keeps the whole rack thin enough to disappear against the inside of the door.
timeLabel: 45 min–1.25 hrs plus glue drying time

steps.title: Cut all the pieces
steps.body: Everything comes off the one 4 ft (48") 1x4, which is a true 1/2" thick and 3-1/2" wide, so most pieces have to be ripped to width as well as crosscut to length. Crosscut two 14-1/2" lengths and one 8" length first, which uses about 37" of the board and leaves room for a mistake. Then rip: from the first 14-1/2" piece take the bottom at 3" wide; from the second take the bottom front at 1-1/2" and the top front at 1", which sit side by side inside the 3-1/2" width. From the 8" piece rip two back verticals 1/2" wide and two strips 1" wide, then crosscut the four 2-1/2" side pieces out of those strips. Note before you start that the listed tools cover crosscutting only — the rips need a table saw, a circular saw with a straightedge, or a hand plane.

steps.title: Glue the base
steps.body: Stand one 2-1/2" side piece at each end of the 3"-deep bottom with its 1" face against the bottom, then glue and spring-clamp them there. These two pieces set the depth of every slot and hold the front rails clear of the bottom, so keep them square to the bottom and flush with its ends — a rack that is out of square holds the boards at a lean inside the cabinet door. Give the glue about 30 minutes before you handle the assembly, and its full cure time before you load it.

steps.title: Add the back verticals
steps.body: Glue the two 8" back verticals upright at the back corners, one against each side piece — at 1/2" square they set the rack's 8" height and are what the cutting boards lean against. Clamp them plumb to the bottom, since any lean here becomes a gap between the rack and the cabinet door once it is mounted. If you can, notch the side pieces to receive the verticals before gluing: it roughly doubles the glue surface on the smallest parts in the whole rack, which is exactly where a glue-only assembly fails.

steps.title: Close up the rack
steps.body: Glue the 14-1/2" bottom front rail across the front on its 1-1/2" face, then the 14-1/2" top front rail on its 1" face higher up — between them and the back verticals, each cutting board is captured top and bottom instead of leaning loose. Add the two remaining 2-1/2" side pieces at the upper front corners to tie the top rail back to the verticals. Clamp the whole thing and check it stays square and flat across its 14-1/2" width, because it has to sit flush against a cabinet door. Let the glue cure fully before you sand or finish it.

steps.title: Finish and mount
steps.body: Stain and seal with polyurethane, then mount inside a cabinet door.
